sentences.text: I don't think so.
No, I don't think so.
They were, they were young people.
Mostly in the ghettos were young people.
The old one dying.
They took them away.
And the young, they find another man, find a girl.
They begin to love each other and to be together in the misery.
You understand?
And they find happiness in the misery-- some happiness.
And they begin to think about how to go out from that.
No.
No.
No, absolute no.
Because everybody was for himself.
If he had another piece of bread, even if he didn't eat, he'd never give it to you.
He'd put it in  the pocket for tomorrow.
Because you never knew what will be tomorrow.
Were very bad times.
Very bad times.
Nothing.
Nothing.
They didn't come in contact with us.
They didn't let them.
And they were very happy, because they took away everything what is ours.
They robbed the houses.
They took away the houses.
They took away everything what was in the houses.
So they were very happy.
Now, we can live without the Jew.
Yeah.
Quietly, they were talking about it.
How can we make-- what can we do to escape of that?
And then the plan came in.
Like that, you cannot escape.
But under the court we will have to build some passage, and we will escape.
And that's what they built.
And not many, but whatever escaped, escaped.
We have, in the parties in the camp, a couple of these-- Sonia Orszman and her husband escaped from the, from the tunnel.
I was already not in the ghetto.
My father is a very smart.
He was very smart.
And he saw-- he was here when he was going out, he had many friends-- Polish.
And they were telling him that they're looking for a solution of the Jewish people.
So he knew, he's with the mother in [NON-ENGLISH], where Lea's house is, yeah?
And they took me to the court-- Which Lea?
Lea-- this Lea pays us.
Frydberg.
They'll live there, because this-- already 40, 45, this was already a lot old people.
Even my mother was not 45 then, and my father.
No, no.
So they began already to talk about the solution, that the ghetto will be finished, quick.
And only the people who are there are in the court, they are able to work, and they will stay alive.
The court in Novogrudok was a court.
The people were there working.
They were working for the German.
They were making these for the horses to go.
[NON-ENGLISH], how you'd say?
Saddles.
So they were working there in the, in the court.
They gave it to him how to make the saddles, and they were doing this.
They were making for these-- shoes for the army.
So they were working there.
And they were sure thinking that they will stay alive till the war will finish.
But this was the only you know-- nothing.
Sure.
Not too many, but a couple.
Because somebody told him that they had too much food there or something to the camp.
And they find food in the-- in this ghetto, in the barracks.
So they took it out, the whole family, and they killed them, because they had potatoes in there.
This is beginning already from the court.
So my father decided with my mother that they have to take me out from the court.
The bread came to us from [NON-ENGLISH], from [NON-ENGLISH], That's where my father, mother.
So they gave it to him money.
Then he brought the bread into the court.
He took off the bread, and he put me in the, in the bread, where the bread was.
And he took me to my parents in the ghetto.
And from then, they were beginning to think how to go out to the forest.
They didn't think about them.
They were thinking all about me.
They were telling me, [NON-ENGLISH], you have to go away.
You will go away.
You will come to, to Israel.
You will tell your brother how-- where we are dying.
You see, they were thinking only about dying.
But they want me to stay alive, to tell everybody, to the children, that how they're dying.
You understand?
I didn't want to think about it, even.
I didn't want to go.
And then the letters began to come from my husband.
He didn't know me.
I didn't know him.
But his sister, [NON-ENGLISH], she once was by us in, in the house.
Something family, something was it.
I don't know.
I never saw her.
But she came once.
And she said to, to my husband in the forest, she said, I was with the Boldos in the house, and I saw a beautiful girl they have.
And why don't you take her into the forest?
My husband was already married.
He had a daughter, too, from three months.
But his wife didn't want to go to the forest.
He said, all the Jew are sitting in the houses.
Only you are afraid you have to go through forest?
I'm not going with the baby to the forest.
And when he went away to the forest, he never was in the ghetto.
Three days he was there with the child.
And after three days, they took all of them to kill.
So he was by himself in the forest.
And first but 12 or 14 people in the forest.
But Lily was already in the forest, because Tuvia left his wife in the city, in the village where he was working and he had a store.
And she didn't want to go with him too through the forest.
So--
Tuvia Bielski.
So he couldn't stay.
He knew that this is-- the German are looking for everybody.
So he went away.
When he went away in the-- he saw Sonia, his second wife.
And he fell in love with her.
Sonia's sister-- Sonia-- Tuvia's wife's sister was married to Lily's father.
And this was the family.
When I came into forest, Zusia was together with them.
Because Sonia was--
Me?
My father-- Zusia wrote two letters-- three letters, two letters.
And he said, he doesn't know what will be with everybody.
But one thing, he's guaranteed that I will be alive after the war.
And they have to believe him that he will take care of me.
And when my mother and father was reading the letter, and she said like that-- I remember it like today-- they are from the forest-- the people from the forest.
And if people from the forest telling you something, you have to believe them.
This was it.
And they said to me, you have to go.
You don't have to sit here with us.
You have to go.
Then already, August 15, in '42, I lost my brother, Jankele.
This was a second action.
And they killed a lot of people.
And he saw that my mother is going to death.
So he took her, took her out from the people what are going for death.
And they've counted them.
And they caught him for the hint.
And they said, give me lady what you took her out.
This is the number.
He said, I don't know nothing about it.
Oh, you don't know?
OK.
So they took him on the truck.
And he was a policeman.
But he didn't die at-- they didn't take him to this.
He was running from the truck, and they shoot him.
How do we know this?
Because from the village, they came and they told us that your son was dead.
He ran from the truck.
We saw this.
And they killed him.
After this, I was already less, you know?
I see that, that the end is coming.
I have to do something for my parents, too.
They asked me, they begged me, I should go away.
I lived in Lea's house-- Lea Frydberg.
My father called a policemen, from the police-- the head of the police.
And he said, listen, I have only one daughter, and I want she should escape.
Tonight is the night.
My husband sent two people with [NON-ENGLISH].
How do you call this with the--
Rifles.
And you have to open up for them.
Do whatever you wanted.
I will give you whatever you wanted.
You know that.
He said, Mr.
Boldo, you don't have to give me.
I know you.
You want Sonia should escape?
He said, yes.
And this was in the-- he opened the gates for us.
Not gates with this.
He cut the wires.
And Lea, because she lived with me, she went out with me together to the forest.
And this was the story from the forest.
We went out.
Probably it was about 12 o'clock.
About 4 o'clock in the morning, we came to the partisan.
How did it go?
With number 11.
People came to pick us up.
Partisan.
Alex send them for me, and Lea went with me.
And--
Alex Zus, my husband.
And they picked us up, and they brought--
In the partisan, in the forest.
In the forest.
Oh, the people what he sent.
He knew-- they knew where to bring me.
And we fall asleep.
We were so tired--
About four hours, probably.
And you know, you hear the, the dogs, and you hear the German talking and everything.
And you don't see nobody.
And it's dark.
And you're afraid.
Of everything, you're afraid.
And I always had afraid of everything.
I was very, very, very careful.
I would-- everything.
It came about 4 o'clock, I was very tired, and I fell asleep under the tree.
And I heard somebody is running like that.
I was thinking that's maybe, you know, a [NON-ENGLISH], a flea, whatever.
And went like that.
No.
There again, something like that.
And I caught the hand.
A hand.
This was the hand for the man what they came to pick me out from to ghetto.
And he was running a little bit to me.
He was [INAUDIBLE].
He was a [NON-ENGLISH], you know?
A barber?
Barber.
And this is the moment.
And I opened-- maybe 20 minutes I was asleep.
I opened the eyes, and I said, oh, no, Sonia.
Nobody will do this to you.
And I was sitting till 4:30, 4:30.
And 4:30, Chaya-- Asael's-- Chaya-- Asael's-- his brother's girlfriends.
Zus's brother's girlfriends was already in the forest with him.
[NON-ENGLISH].
Chaya [NON-ENGLISH].
And she knew me very well.
And she was studying in Wilno.
She was a-- from the Red-- from the [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what-- she was believing in the-- in Stalin.
And she was always running away from them.
And once she came to our house to sleep over.
And she knew me.
A [NON-ENGLISH].
A [NON-ENGLISH].
And I said, where is Chaya?
So they said to me-- somebody said, she will-- about 5 o'clock she's coming out from the, from the, whatever, where she was sleeping.
And that's right.
She came.
And I fall on her.
I said, Chaya, I'm so happy to see you.
She said, I'm very happy to see you, too, Sonia.
I knew that you're coming.
Zus-- Alex and Zissel is Zus.
They were calling him.
His name was Zissel.
And the Jewish-- Alexander-- Alexander Macedonski was very good to the Jews in the time.
So, after then, when a boy was born, they gave it to him the name of Alexander.
So he was Alexander Zissel Bielski.
But we called him-- everybody called him Zus.
And I called him Zus till the end of my-- til the end of his life.
I said, is she telling me that she has a commander, and she's in love with him, and she's very happy?
I said to her, tell me, is there another commander maybe here?
She said, yeah, but you have to be very smart.
I said, what do you mean, I have to be very smart?
Because he's already with somebody.
I said, so.
But never mind, Sonia.
You can do it.
That's what she told me.
About 5:30, I saw a tall man, 6' 2', with a big, big fur coat from the villages they're burning.
The boots were like that.
Everything was beautiful, clean.
He was always a very clean man.
Something.
And so handsome.
But I never saw a man like that.
Never saw a man.
I had afraid of him.
And Chaya said to him, this is Sonia.
Oh, I said, I know, I were expecting them a couple of hour before.
No, they were a little late.
And this was the beginning.
And he came by 6 o'clock.
And he gave to me-- you want a drink?
I said yes.
So he brought me water and honey.
And I drink the water and honey.
He was very polite, very nice to me.
And he was sleeping near Lily, my sister-in-law.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] But after, the night after, he took out the coat from her and he put the coat on me.
And this was the end of that.
Yes, Zus wrote the letter to me.
But it's nothing have to do-- he didn't have nothing to do with her.
But they were sleeping together.
Sonia was very smart.
She knew that she wanted all the Bielskis to have around her.
She was the queen of the Bielskis.
She wanted to be the queen of the Bielskis.
And I came in, and I took away Zus.
So she's already not the queen.
You understand?
Do you understand?
How was she related to him, again?
She had Tuvia.
Sonia had Tuvia.
So she was his sister-in-law.
My sister-in-law.
And Zus-- she want to put Lily with Zus together.
And I came in, and I took away Zus.
I didn't take away.
He went by himself.
This was Tuvia's brother.
Lily was the other sister.
She was-- the, the-- her mother-- her sister was married to Lily's, Lily's father.
But this was a second marriage.
They were not, not related, nothing.
This was a second mother.
You understand?
A [NON-ENGLISH].
Stepmother.
This is it.
But she was very mad on me, Sonia.
From the first day what I came in, she was so mad on me, terrible.
I came in-- like a princess I came to the forest.
I had four from Zakopane.
From Zakopane-- this is in the south of Poland, there is Zakopane-- [NON-ENGLISH], Zakopane-- where they're making the furs and everything is with the hand he made it.
So I came with a sweater, with a fur, with [NON-ENGLISH], with boots.
[NON-ENGLISH], you know?
And she was probably-- I don't know if she was jealous or something.
I don't know.
I don't think so.
She was a very beautiful, beautiful lady.
And she was older than me.
How big was the group of partisans when you joined?
There were probably about 21 or 20 people in the partisan when I came in with Lea Frydberg.
Not more.
Oh, this was Stara Huta.
Stara Huta.
This is near Novogrudok-- Lida.
This is the place where all the partisan ours.
This is the forest of [NON-ENGLISH].
Yeah.
And this was between Novogrudok and Lida.
There I came.
No.
There were three brothers, and Aharon was the small one.
There were no leaders.
All of them, the three of them, whatever they decided, they did it.
Nobody couldn't do nothing about it.
Together, like that.
Tuvia, Asael, and Zusia.
Till they begin people to coming more and more.
Aharon [INAUDIBLE] 13 years, 12 years.
He was with the brothers.
But they were together.
Nobody could something say something about them or whatever.
All of them, they were sitting, staying on the side, and they decided what to do and what.
And the people begin to coming.
Oh, the-- from where to take the bread.
[INAUDIBLE] to take the this, or how to go to the, to the villages to ask for bread.
Nobody wanted to give, but they had afraid of the three brothers.
They were telling the German-- the German give a lot of money for the heads for them.
But they knew that if they will touch one, all of the village will go on fire.
They had afraid of them.
From Novogrudok-- from the Novogrudok ghetto, from Lida ghetto.
From all over the places where Jewish life was, they came in.
They were sending people for young people should come to fight the German.
The brothers.
The brothers.
And they came in.
Not too many in beginning.
Later, came in more and more.
When the German begin to killing them, they were running away.
They came.
By the end, we had 1,200 people.
It was young people-- mostly was young.
Later begin to come a little older-- a little older.
They had a wife, so they came with the wife.
But they knew, too-- they knew very well the places.
Because they lived between the Polish neighborhood.
You understand?
So this was help, too.
They were coming and going to take away a horse that they [INAUDIBLE].
And chicken-- taking away eggs.
Who decided who can come into the camp?
Nobody decided.
They decided as many as we can to take Jew in, they should come.
They were not talking about children.
Very little children came in.
Well, maybe it was about three, four children came in.
Because the children were killed by the German.
We have now a couple of children what they burried in the forest.
They were five years.
Now there's one from the biggest professor in Toronto.
At ?] [? teeth. ?] He's making the implant.
We are going always to him.
He was five years when the war was finished.
Druck-- D-R-U-C-K-- Druck.
Yeah.
We have another one, what he's in the head of Maimonodes.
He was only 14 years old.
A gynecologist.
We had a couple of these--
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Jewelewicz.
Jewelewicz, yeah.
Yes
The leadership began already.
Yes.
You see, the three of them, there couldn't be three commanders.
One has to be the commander.
So they decided-- in the villages like that, it's always like that.
The oldest brother, he is the head of the [INAUDIBLE].
[NON-ENGLISH] was the head of the partisan from  the whole [NON-ENGLISH], from all over.
He was sent out from Moscow.
And he was the commander from all the partisan platoon.
Yes.
With Zus?
I was with Zus as soon as I came to the forest.
He told me that he likes me-- not he loves me, he likes me.
And he would like-- he would like I should stay with him.
I said, I was thinking about it, for a minute.
I didn't think too, too much.
I said yes, I will stay with you.
But you have to promise me to take it out from the ghetto my father and mother.
He said, then, then I will be with you.
And this took about three, four days.
And I saw my father and mother in the forest.
After the six, seven days, the whole ghetto was-- liquidation of the ghetto.
Nobody went out already.
And I decided to stay with Zus, because I promise him.
But I didn't have the smallest-- I didn't-- I was not thinking that to stay with him, that's mean that I have to go with him to bed.
And this was the contract, that I have to stay with him, to go with him, to be like a wife to him.
And it let us go.
He didn't touch me.
He didn't He said, when?
I say, OK, so come.
So it took about two, three months.
And I saw that all the people, they're coming, they're coming young, beautiful girls.
Always they were coming to him with everything.
With everything.
There is-- I have friends there in the partisan.
And they was telling me, Sonia, you know, he was with her, there and there.
They were telling me.
Now I had a friend, [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH], my friends from school.
She was telling me, are you [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what you're doing?
I said, what I'm doing?
[NON-ENGLISH]?
I said-- Meaning what?
That he went with this-and-this in a hooter on a farm.
I said, he was home.
He came about 11 o'clock.
So till 11 o'clock, he did whatever he needed.
And I begin to be very jealous.
Listen to me.
I didn't fall in love with him.
I was very jealous, because everybody likes him.
And I couldn't fall in love with him.
I was afraid of him.
I don't know.
But my parents had everything.
They had the bread and butter and everything.
I had everybody.
They were calling for work.
I didn't go.
Tuvia was calling all the [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what's [NON-ENGLISH]?
All the girls.
The [NON-ENGLISH].
Go to work.
Go do something.
And I never went to work, because I didn't feel that I am a [NON-ENGLISH], that I am a [NON-ENGLISH].
[NON-ENGLISH], this was his silly language, OK?
This was his language.
Loose.
Loose.
Mostly they were loose, true.
I don't blame that either, because a woman, just girl, she was by herself, she needed a pair of shoes, she needs a pair-- a sweater.
She needs something, so she was looking for a man.
So now this man, so another man, till she finds something what she liked it.
Mostly everybody find--
Oh, yeah.
They, they lived together with the man.
And this is it.
